Model Comparison

Simple (LOS+AGE+ED) 3 2,109,971 2,109,920 0.3458

About This Model

What was done

Three linear regression models were estimated on federal employment salary data. Variable selection used stepwise regression penalised by BIC (k = log(n)), which applies a stricter parsimony penalty than AIC, especially at large sample sizes. The bidirectional search both adds and removes variables at each step.

Why BIC over AIC

With over 230,000 observations, AIC tends to retain more variables than necessary. BIC scaling by log(n) makes each additional predictor substantially more costly, producing a leaner model less likely to overfit noise in the training data.

Interpreting R²

The selected model explains approximately 56% of variance in salary. The remaining 44% reflects individual negotiation, within-grade pay steps, performance bonuses, and other factors not present in the dataset.

Residual Analysis

Row

Residual Analysis — Residuals (Actual minus Predicted) reveal where the model succeeds and where it struggles. Ideally residuals are centred at zero with symmetric spread and no systematic patterns across agencies or salary levels.

Row

Residual Distribution

Predicted vs. Actual

Row

Residual Distribution — Interpretation

📊

Residuals are roughly centred at zero as expected from OLS. The mild right skew is typical for salary data — a small number of very high earners are harder to predict, pulling the distribution rightward. A log transformation of SALARY may reduce this skew in future iterations.

Predicted vs. Actual — Interpretation

🎯

Points cluster along the 45° line confirming the model captures the central salary trend. The spread widens at higher salaries — a classic sign of heteroscedasticity. Senior and specialised roles have more individual variance not explained by the administrative predictors.

Row

Residuals by Agency

Row

Residuals by Agency — Interpretation

🏛️

Agencies whose violins sit above zero are systematically under-predicted. Those below zero are over-predicted. Wide violins indicate high within-agency variance in prediction error, which may reflect heterogeneous job types or pay scales not fully captured by the current predictors.

Salary Patterns

Row

Salary Patterns — Exploring how salary varies with length of service, agency, and education level, and how well the model captures those patterns.

Row

Salary vs. Length of Service

Row

Length of Service — Interpretation

📈

Each line shows the agency-specific salary growth rate with seniority. Steeper slopes indicate a stronger seniority pay premium within that agency. The diverging slopes across agencies supports including both LOS and AGY as separate predictors. An interaction term LOS:AGY would allow the model to capture these differing slopes directly.

Row

Predicted vs. Actual by Agency

Salary by Education Level

Row

Predicted vs. Actual — Interpretation

🎻

Paired violins compare actual (blue) and predicted (green) salary distributions per agency. A narrower predicted violin indicates the model compresses extreme values — capturing the centre but missing the tails, a known limitation of OLS minimising squared error.

Education Level — Interpretation

🎓

The monotonically increasing trend confirms education is a meaningful positive predictor of federal salary. The steeper rise at higher EDLVL codes suggests non-linear returns to education. A quadratic term or factor encoding of EDLVL may improve fit beyond the current linear treatment.

Contract Spend

Row

Contract Spend — Monthly federal contract obligations by agency, compared against salary overhead and CPI-U inflation rate on an annual basis.

Row

Row

Monthly Contract Spend by Agency

Row

Monthly Spend — Interpretation

📋

Each panel shows the monthly federal contract obligation for that component. The loess trend line (dotted green) smooths over month-to-month volatility to surface directional changes. End-of-fiscal-year spikes (September) are common across all agencies as remaining appropriations are obligated before the budget year closes.

Row

Avg Annual Contract Spend vs Salary vs Inflation

Row

Annual Comparison — Interpretation

⚖️

Annual averages smooth out seasonal noise to expose multi-year trends. Salary overhead (dashed red) and CPI-U inflation (dot-dash gold) are rescaled to the contract spend axis for visual comparison only — the left axis label reflects contract dollars. Periods where contract spend grows faster than inflation suggest real spending increases beyond cost-of-living pressures.
